title: Topography-based navigation in a millikelvin scanning tunneling microscope using binary-encoded position markers
authors: R. Fischer-Süßlin, R. Hartmann, T. Kandra, E. Scheer
abstract: We present a compact millikelvin scanning tunneling microscope (STM) operating at 270mK with topographic navigation to micron-scale targets. Two piezoelectric low-temperature nanopositioners extend the accessible sample area, while a multi-stage copper powder and capillary filter scheme preserves millikelvin energy resolution, verified by BCS spectroscopy on aluminium thin films. A lithographically fabricated binary-encoded gold pattern encodes unique 16-bit coordinates in 4x4 pixels of 200nm$\times$200nm each. We demonstrate absolute positioning across a 350$\times$350$\mathrmμ$m$^2$ area from a single STM scan. Requiring only a single lithography step and no hardware modifications to existing STM setups, the navigation system provides a versatile platform for scanning tunneling spectroscopy of nanoflakes and nanoscale devices.
summary: This paper presents an advanced, compact millikelvin STM setup designed for high-precision spectroscopy on nanoscale targets. It introduces a novel topography-based navigation system that uses a lithographically patterned binary grid to determine absolute coordinates across a large area. This allows researchers to reliably map and study multiple distinct quantum regions on a single sample without external optical guidance.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving precise, absolute positioning for Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) and Spectroscopy (STS) on micron-scale targets while operating at millikelvin temperatures, overcoming limitations like thermal drift and lack of optical access.
main_result: The authors successfully integrated a topography-based navigation system using a lithographically patterned binary-encoded gold grid with piezoelectric nanopositioners, enabling absolute positioning across a $350	imes350 \mu	ext{m}^2$ area from a single STM scan.
method: The system uses a patterned substrate where unique 16-bit coordinates are encoded by the topography (elevated pixels = '1'). An initial STM scan reads this pattern, which is then interpreted by software to guide the nanopositioners to the desired target location for subsequent spectroscopy.
model_or_system: The platform is a compact millikelvin STM operating at $\sim 270$ mK. Positioning is achieved via two piezoelectric nanopositioners, and the absolute location is read from a lithographically fabricated $4	imes4$ array of $200 	ext{ nm}	imes 200 	ext{ nm}$ binary-encoded gold markers.
key_observables: Absolute position ($	ext{x}, 	ext{y}$ coordinates), differential conductance ($dI/dV$), and energy resolution (verified via BCS spectroscopy on Al).
important_parameters: Operating temperature ($\sim 270 	ext{ mK}$), scan area ($350	imes350 \mu	ext{m}^2$), pixel size ($200 	ext{ nm}$), and energy resolution ($20 \mu	ext{eV}$).
main_assumptions: The system assumes stable thermal anchoring and relies on the ability of the STM tip to enter the tunneling range to read the coordinate pattern, which is noted as a potential risk.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the STM head components, the measured $dI/dV$ spectra for Al samples, the SEM image of the fabricated navigation grid, and a schematic detailing the topography-based navigation process.
paper_structure: The paper details the physical setup (STM, nanopositioners, filtering), describes the novel topography-based navigation method using binary encoding, demonstrates the absolute positioning capability, and verifies the system's performance using spectroscopic measurements.
why_interesting: While the core physics is condensed matter (superconductivity, nanoscale devices), the engineering solution for precise, low-temperature positioning is highly relevant to any experiment requiring stable, absolute addressing of quantum systems, bridging experimental physics and advanced instrumentation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Photograph of the STM head showing (1) the cop- per slider with tip and (2) the sample plate on top of the (3) two linear nanopostioners. A sample heater (4) prevents adsorption on the sample, and for thermal coupling the sam- ple plate is connected to the gold-plated copper shielding (5). Additionally to the thermometers of the cryostat insert, we mounted a sample thermometer (6). For radiation shielding, the STM head is closed with (7) small copper elements.

FIG. 2. Measured differential conductance dI/dV of an PtIr tip and an evaporated film with 200 nm aluminium and 10 nm copper for different temperatures. The spectra were recorded with a modulation voltage Vmod = 30 µV , a stabilization cur- rent I = 400 pA at V = 3 mV.

FIG. 3. False-colored SEM image of a sample with the navi- gation system on top. The design is based on the dual system with a 4×4 grid, shown in white, of 200 nm×200 nm pixels that are either 1 (elevated by 10nm, blue) or 0 (no elevation). For a clear separation of grids, the border (orange) is also elevated and has an antisymmetric form in x and y for clear orientation of the pattern.

FIG. 4. Topography-based navigation process. STM scans (1- 4) at room temperature and the software (big image) showing the exact position in the pattern and therewith on the sam- ple. Between the STM scans the attocube nanopositioners are moved.
